label: Iguala — Ayotzinapa Attack
sub: Site of the September 26, 2014 attack on 43 Ayotzinapa normalistas. Municipal police under Mayor José Luis Abarca intercepted student buses at multiple points across the city, handing students to the Guerreros Unidos cartel.

label: Escuela Normal Rural 'Raúl Isidro Burgos', Tixtla
sub: Home campus of the 43 disappeared students. The Ayotzinapa Rural Teachers' College has historically trained teachers for poor rural communities in Guerrero. Students departed from here on the night of September 26, 2014.

label: Cocula Municipal Dump — 'Verdad Histórica' Site
sub: The PGR's official 'verdad histórica' (Jan 2015) claimed students were incinerated here by Guerreros Unidos. GIEI's first report (Sep 2015) concluded this was 'scientifically impossible' — no evidence of large-scale fire was found at the site.

label: Palacio Nacional, Mexico City
sub: Seat of the executive branch. Site of the Pacto por México signing (Dec 2, 2012) and the November 30, 2018 presidential transfer from Peña Nieto to AMLO.

label: Los Pinos — Presidential Residence
sub: Official presidential residence during the Peña Nieto sexenio. Site of Donald Trump's surprise visit on September 1, 2016 — a diplomatic meeting widely criticized domestically as humiliating for Mexico.

label: Altiplano Prison (CEFERESO No. 1), Almoloya de Juárez
sub: Mexico's maximum-security federal prison in Estado de México. El Chapo was held here after his first capture (2014), escaped through a 1.5 km tunnel on July 11, 2015, and was returned here after recapture in January 2016 pending extradition to the U.S.

label: Mazatlán, Sinaloa — First Chapo Capture
sub: SEMAR marines captured Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán Loera at the Miramar condominium complex on February 22, 2014, without a shot fired. The arrest was the most celebrated security achievement of the Peña Nieto administration.

label: Los Mochis, Sinaloa — Chapo Recapture
sub: SEMAR marines raided a safe house in Los Mochis on January 8, 2016, recapturing El Chapo after a six-month manhunt. Chapo briefly escaped through a sewer system but was captured on a road outside the city.

label: Ciudad Juárez — Chapo Extradition Point
sub: El Chapo was flown from Ciudad Juárez to the United States on January 19, 2017, transferred to U.S. DEA custody. He was subsequently tried and convicted in Brooklyn federal court in February 2019, sentenced to life plus 30 years.

label: Casa Blanca — Paseo de la Reforma 150, CDMX
sub: The MXN $54 million mansion purchased by First Lady Angélica Rivera from Grupo Higa — a construction firm with major government contracts under Peña Nieto. Exposed by Aristegui Noticias on November 5, 2014; central to the Casa Blanca corruption scandal.

label: Pemex HQ — Paseo de la Reforma 333, CDMX
sub: Corporate headquarters of Petróleos Mexicanos (Pemex). Central to the energy reform and the Odebrecht corruption scandal, where CEO Emilio Lozoya Austin channeled bribe payments to the PRI's 2012 campaign.

label: Nochixtlán, Oaxaca — 2016 CNTE Massacre
sub: Site of the June 19, 2016 confrontation between Federal Police and CNTE teacher protesters. Eight civilians were killed and over 100 injured when police opened fire on demonstrators blocking the Oaxaca-Mexico City highway. CNDH cited excessive force.

label: Juchitán de Zaragoza, Oaxaca — M8.2 Earthquake
sub: Town severely damaged in the September 7, 2017 magnitude 8.2 earthquake — the strongest in Mexico in 100 years. The historic town center was effectively destroyed; 72 people died in Oaxaca state alone. Reconstruction was slow and politically contentious.

label: Rébsamen School Collapse — Tlalpan, CDMX
sub: Enrique Rébsamen primary school collapsed in the September 19, 2017 M7.1 earthquake, killing 26 children and 6 adults. The disaster became a symbol of the earthquake tragedy and raised questions about building code enforcement and construction corruption.

label: Texcoco — NAIM Airport Construction Site
sub: Site of the Nuevo Aeropuerto Internacional de México (NAIM) Texcoco project — a $13 billion airport to replace Mexico City's saturated Benito Juárez airport. Construction reached 30%+ completion before AMLO's citizen consultation voted 70% to cancel the project in October 2018.

label: Xalapa, Veracruz — Duarte Corruption Hub
sub: Capital of Veracruz state under Governor Javier Duarte (PRI), who embezzled billions in public health and education funds through ghost companies before fleeing to Guatemala in December 2016. One of the largest PRI gubernatorial corruption scandals of the era.

label: Acapulco, Guerrero — Violence Epicenter
sub: Acapulco held the title of world's most violent city by homicide rate in multiple years during the Peña Nieto sexenio. Guerreros Unidos and competing cartels contested control of the port city and drug trafficking routes throughout this period.

label: Reynosa, Tamaulipas — Cartel Violence Border City
sub: Major border city in Tamaulipas plagued by CDG (Cartel del Golfo) and Los Zetas violence throughout the sexenio. Tamaulipas was designated a high-security zone; journalists operating in the state faced extreme danger.

label: Tlatlaya, Estado de México — Army Massacre Site
sub: On June 30, 2014, Mexican army soldiers killed 22 suspected cartel members at a warehouse in Tlatlaya. Subsequent investigation showed at least 12 of the 22 were executed after surrendering — not killed in combat as initially claimed. CNDH documented extrajudicial executions; few soldiers were ultimately convicted.

label: Tula Refinery (Miguel Hidalgo), Hidalgo
sub: Major Pemex refinery in Hidalgo state, affected by the 'huachicoleo' (fuel theft) epidemic that cost Pemex billions annually. Fuel theft from pipelines became a national crisis during the late Peña Nieto years, partly enabled by cartel infiltration of Pemex infrastructure.

label: Chetumal, Quintana Roo — Borge Corruption
sub: Capital of Quintana Roo under Governor Roberto Borge Angulo (PRI), who was arrested in Panama in June 2017 and extradited to Mexico. Borge sold state properties to criminal associates and embezzled hundreds of millions in public funds. He was sentenced to 8 years in prison.

label: Culiacán, Sinaloa — Sinaloa Cartel Heartland
sub: Capital of Sinaloa state and the base of the Sinaloa Cartel led by El Chapo. The cartel's homeland territory was largely spared federal pressure under the Peña Nieto security strategy, which critics accused of selective targeting of rival organizations.

label: Río San Juan — Ayotzinapa Remains Disposal Site
sub: River near Cocula where PGR claimed Guerreros Unidos dumped plastic bags containing incinerated remains of the 43 students. A bone fragment from Alexander Mora Venancio was recovered here in December 2014 by Argentine forensic experts (EAAF) and became the only confirmed DNA identification among the 43.

label: Veracruz Port — Key Oil Export Terminal
sub: Mexico's main oil export and container port. Key to Pemex crude oil exports and the economic impact of falling oil production and prices during 2014–2016. The port state was also the epicenter of the Javier Duarte governance scandal.

label: Morelia, Michoacán — Autodefensas & Federal Intervention
sub: Capital of Michoacán, site of the federal government's major intervention in 2014 to dismantle both the Knights Templar cartel and the self-defense (autodefensa) militias that had risen to combat them. The operation was a centerpiece of Peña Nieto's security strategy and a test case for re-establishing state control.
